name: Pediatric cardiac mitochondrial transplantation was associated with ECMO separation
summary: The strongest treated-human efficacy-adjacent signal is a small pediatric acute ischemia-reperfusion pilot, not aging evidence.
statement: In a retrospective pediatric postcardiotomy ischemia-reperfusion cohort, revascularization plus autologous mitochondrial transplantation was associated with successful ECMO separation in 8 of 10 treated patients versus 4 of 14 controls, better ventricular strain after ECMO separation, shorter median functional recovery, and fewer cardiovascular events.
population_or_model: Pediatric patients on postcardiotomy ECMO for severe refractory cardiogenic shock after ischemia-reperfusion injury
time_horizon: Clinical follow-up around revascularization, ECMO separation, and early recovery
quantitative_note: The paper reports ECMO separation of 80% versus 29% (P = .02), median time to functional recovery of 2 versus 9 days (P = .02), and cardiovascular events of 20% versus 79% (P < .01) for mitochondrial transplantation versus control.
caveats: This is non-randomized, retrospective, single-center, pediatric, and acute injury focused.
The result does not establish broad aging, older-adult, or chronic mitochondrial-dysfunction benefit.
The technology has specialized surgical workflow, sourcing, dosing, route, and quality-control constraints.
